summary: User policy for legacy Windows Copilot. Microsoft documents the same user policy path and value name that the app writes, but the policy is deprecated and does not apply to the newer Copilot experience rolling out on some newer Windows builds.

setting.name: Windows Copilot
setting.casual_explanation: Windows Copilot is the built-in AI assistant experience in Windows. This policy decides whether that experience is available to the signed-in user.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they do not want AI features in the shell, want a simpler desktop, or need a managed baseline that removes Copilot entry points.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off removes the Copilot icon and user access to Copilot. Leaving it at the Windows default allows Copilot when Microsoft makes it available to that user.
setting.professional_notes: WindowsCopilot.admx and the WindowsAI Policy CSP map TurnOffWindowsCopilot to HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsCopilot\TurnOffWindowsCopilot with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. Microsoft now marks this policy as deprecated and explicitly says it is not for the newer Copilot experience rolling out on some Windows builds.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the Copilot policy for this user, so Copilot remains available when Microsoft exposes it to that user.

allowed_values.label: Copilot turned off
allowed_values.meaning: The user cannot use Windows Copilot, and the Copilot icon does not appear on the taskbar.

allowed_values.label: Copilot allowed
allowed_values.meaning: This policy does not turn Copilot off, so the user can use Copilot when it is available to them.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes TurnOffWindowsCopilot = 1 for the current user, which matches the documented turn-off state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows user-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 11 systems exposing the Windows Copilot policy

states.rationale: The policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: When the policy is not configured, Copilot is available when Microsoft exposes it to that user.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want Windows to decide whether Copilot is available
avoid_for: Managed or privacy-focused systems that want Copilot gone
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its ordinary Copilot availability behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You explicitly do not want the Copilot UI or AI entry point.
tradeoffs: Copilot can remain available when Microsoft enables it for the user.
No hard policy block is enforced.

recommended_profiles.label: No Copilot
intended_for: People who want a simpler shell without Copilot
avoid_for: Users who actively want Copilot features

states.rationale: Turn off Windows Copilot through user policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Copilot and its taskbar entry point removed for this user.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to keep Copilot available in Windows.
tradeoffs: The Copilot icon and user access are removed.
The user loses the built-in Copilot experience.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_WindowsCopilot Policy CSP: TurnOffWindowsCopilot
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that turns Windows Copilot off for the user, including the behavior and registry mapping.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft WindowsCopilot.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps TurnOffWindowsCopilot to SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsCopilot\TurnOffWindowsCopilot with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft WindowsCopilot.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy prevents users from using Copilot and removes the Copilot icon from the taskbar, while disabling or not configuring it allows Copilot when available.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes TurnOffWindowsCopilot = 1 for the current user.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Microsoft documents TurnOffWindowsCopilot directly in the WindowsAI Policy CSP and maps it to SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsCopilot\TurnOffWindowsCopilot with 0 as the default enabled state and 1 as disabled. The app writes that exact user policy surface, but Microsoft also marks the policy deprecated and says it doesn't govern the newer Copilot experience on some Windows builds.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Default Value 0; Allowed values: 0 (Default) Enable Copilot. 1 Disable Copilot. Group policy mapping: Registry Key Name SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsCopilot; Registry Value Name TurnOffWindowsCopilot. Note: This policy is deprecated and may be removed in a future release. The TurnOffWindowsCopilot policy isn't for the new Copilot experience that's in some Windows Insider builds and that will be gradually rolling out to Windows 11 and Windows 10 devices.
validation_proof.notes: The WindowsAI Policy CSP page explicitly names the exact user policy path and value name the app writes, defines the 0/1 semantics, and includes the deprecation and scope caveat for the newer Copilot experience.
